data.title: Kforce expects Q2 revenue of $344M-$352M and EPS of $0.67-$0.75 as management signals mid-single-digit y/y growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kforce (KFRC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Joseph Liberatore said, "We are extremely pleased to have successfully driven results in the first quarter that again exceeded our expectations from both a revenue and profitability perspective," and added that first-quarter momentum "has continued to strengthen, resulting in year-over-year revenue growth for the first time in several years."

  • CEO Liberatore linked demand to clients’ flexibility needs, saying, "Companies are increasingly turning to flexible talent strategies to move forward on significant backlog of high-priority technology initiatives, especially in the age of artificial intelligence where CEOs remain cautious to add permanent headcount," while also citing macro volatility, including "heightened geopolitical uncertainty, including the conflict involving Iran" and "significant volatility in the global energy markets."

  • COO David Kelly reported "Total revenues of $330.4 million," and described Q1 as better than typical seasonality, stating, "Our results were driven by a combination of lower levels of project ends and a faster-than-normal rebound in new assignment activity." He added that client activity was broad, with "year-over-year growth in 6 of our top 10 sectors," and highlighted pipeline momentum: "our data and AI pipeline is up nearly 50% year-over-year."

  • CFO Jeffrey Hackman said, "First quarter revenue of $330.4 million exceeded our expectations and earnings per share of $0.46 was above the high end of our guidance," and tied margin performance to pricing and mix, stating, "The success we have had expanding our margin profile can be attributed to our teams pricing more effectively with clients to more appropriately reflect the value of our services and the benefit of higher quality business that we have been strategically driving."

Outlook

  • The transcript did not provide usable analyst estimates in a valid format for a rules-based comparison; management guidance is presented on a stand-alone basis.

  • CFO Hackman guided Q2 revenue to "be in the range of $344 million to $352 million" and EPS "between $0.67 and $0.75," adding, "Our guidance assumes a stable operating environment and excludes the potential impact of any unusual or nonrecurring items."

  • CEO Liberatore said first-month Q2 trends were improving, noting, "our trajectory has continued to improve in the first month of the second quarter, which we expect will lead to accelerating year-over-year growth in Q2 in the mid-single digits."

  • CFO Hackman reiterated the longer-term profitability framework, saying, "We are increasingly confident in our ability to generate at least 8% operating margin when annual revenues return to $1.7 billion."

Financial Results

  • CFO Hackman reported gross margin of "27.3%" and said it was helped by Flex performance, stating, "Overall gross margins of 27.3% were up 60 basis points on a year-over-year basis due to expanding Flex margins."

  • CFO Hackman said SG&A as a percentage of revenue was "23.2%" and attributed the year-over-year increase primarily to incentives, stating it "was primarily driven by greater performance-based compensation due to the higher levels of financial performance we have been successful delivering in 2026."

  • CFO Hackman detailed capital returns and leverage, saying, "$18.6 million in capital" was returned via "dividends of $6.8 million and share repurchases of approximately $11.8 million," and added, "This resulted in an increase in net debt to $90.2 million from $64.3 million."

  • CFO Hackman cited cash flow expectations, stating, "Operating cash flows were negative $4.1 million" in Q1 and, "We expect positive operating cash flows of approximately $20 million in Q2."

Q&A

  • Mark Marcon, Baird: Asked about trends by vertical (including financial services) and whether guide strength reflected new wins or client gains; COO David Kelly said, "across industries, we're seeing either stability or growth pretty broadly," adding financial services showed "a little bit of seasonal decline" with "reasonable... stability," while citing demand indicators including client visits "up nearly 10%" and job orders "up nearly 20%."

  • Mark Marcon, Baird: Asked how India delivery is affecting share capture and the margin implications; COO Kelly said the blended model is in use with "60% of our 25 largest clients" but remains "a very small percentage of the revenue stream," and CEO Joseph Liberatore emphasized talent availability, saying, "real shortage in the U.S. We're definitely seeing much more talent availability, especially from an AI standpoint."

  • Mark Marcon, Baird: Pressed on whether offshore is margin accretive despite lower bill rates; CFO Jeffrey Hackman said Flex margin improvement was driven by spread expansion and higher-quality work, noting consulting margin delta "400 to 600 basis points," and added, "as that business continues to scale, we expect that to lead to enrichment at the Flex margin line," while COO Kelly cautioned, "The impact on flexible margins from the offshore facility are nominal."

  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: Asked what share of demand reflects roles that didn’t exist five years ago and how the model adapts; CEO Liberatore answered, "virtually almost 100% of what we're doing today didn't exist 5 years ago" because roles are "being augmented with certain skills in AI."

  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: Asked what the AI innovation studio entails; CEO Liberatore said clients want prototypes, explaining, "Organizations really want something that's tangible, that is a prototype, a working model," and tied it to delivery leverage, adding, "We've established what we call AI pods in India."

  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: Asked if AI pods create repeatable solutions with different pricing and whether repetition yields higher margins; CEO Liberatore said, "the tools in themselves are repeatable" but added, "we're not a product-based organization," and on scaling margins said, "Hypothetically, that would be the case" while noting, "We don't have the practical experience."

  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: Asked if buybacks could outpace cash flow given valuation; CFO Hackman said, "we were very comfortable outstripping the operating cash flows certainly in the first quarter," and added, "you're not going to see us get super aggressive and super wild."

  • Joshua Chan, UBS: Asked whether a staffing recovery can occur without a broader GDP recession and about spread drivers; CEO Liberatore said, "for professionally oriented roles, there has been a job recession," while CFO Hackman said margin gains were execution and mix driven and, "I do not believe that it's a competitive change in the marketplace."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but probing, with questions centered on whether the improved guide reflects durable demand, share gains, and whether AI/offshore can structurally lift margins (e.g., Mark Marcon’s focus on share and India mix; Tobey Sommer’s focus on buybacks and cycle durability).

  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and confident in trajectory, with CEO Liberatore saying, "we remain optimistic" and, "I feel confident in our trajectory," while responses in Q&A stayed measured on unproven monetization of repeatable AI work, stating, "time will tell" and "we don't have the practical experience."

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing around a "persistently weak and largely frozen labor market," management in Q1 emphasized realized improvement and acceleration signals, including Liberatore’s expectation for "accelerating year-over-year growth" in Q2.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1, management shifted from Q4’s emphasis on restructuring charges and cost streamlining to execution-driven growth and mix-led margin improvement, while maintaining the same longer-term target framework around "8% operating margin" at "$1.7 billion" in annual revenues.

  • Guidance language moved from Q4’s Q1 range of "$324 million to $332 million" and EPS of "$0.37" to "$0.45" to Q1’s Q2 outlook of "$344 million to $352 million" and EPS of "$0.67" to "$0.75," alongside more explicit commentary that year-over-year growth is expected to "accelerat[e]" in Q2.

  • Analyst focus remained consistent quarter to quarter around AI-driven demand, India delivery scaling, and margin durability, but Q1 questions leaned more into quantifying share capture and capital allocation intensity given the stronger near-term guide.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Liberatore flagged macro uncertainty, citing "a generally softer labor market for professionally oriented roles" and "heightened geopolitical uncertainty" driving energy-market volatility; he said, "clients are focused on agility" and positioned flexible workforce solutions as the mitigation.

  • CFO Hackman noted cash flow sensitivity in Q1, stating operating cash flows were "negative $4.1 million" due to specific outflows and collections timing, while expecting normalization in Q2.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a turning point back to year-over-year revenue growth, tied to clients restarting delayed technology work through flexible talent and consulting-led engagements. For Q2, management guided to $344 million to $352 million in revenue and $0.67 to $0.75 in EPS while maintaining the longer-term goal of at least 8% operating margin at $1.7 billion of annual revenue, alongside continued capital returns and a stated intent to remain opportunistic but not overly aggressive on leverage-driven buybacks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump administration ends two more offshore wind projects
data.description:
The Trump administration said Monday it reached a deal to end two more ‌U.S. offshore wind leases - one in the Atlantic and one in the Pacific - in exchange for $885M in pledged investments in domestic oil, natural gas, or liquefied natural gas.

The Bluepoint Wind project off the coasts of New York and New Jersey was being developed by Ocean Winds, a joint venture between France's Engie (ENGIY) and Portugal's EDP Renewables (EDPFY) (EDRVF), and BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners, which will relinquish their leases and be reimbursed for costs in exchange for investing $765M in a U.S. liquefied natural gas facility.

On the west coast, Golden State Wind, which was in the early stages of developing a floating offshore wind project, agreed to cancel its lease off Morro Bay, California, in exchange for $120M in lease fees after it invests a similar amount in oil and gas, energy infrastructure or LNG projects.

The agreements are similar to a deal announced last month in which the U.S. government released TotalEnergies and its partners from $1B in offshore wind leases to redirect investment toward oil and natural gas projects in the U.S.

ETF: (FAN)

data.title: Business First targets 1.25% ROAA end-of-year run rate while integrating Progressive and adding bankers in Houston
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Business First Bancshares (BFST) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "This was one of, if not the best, first quarters that we have had as a company," said (Chairman, President & CEO David Melville), adding, "We continue to improve earnings, strengthen capital levels and improve quality of our liquidity posture" while "consummating" the Progressive Bank acquisition and making "nonacquisitive investments."
  • On M&A integration and market positioning, (CEO Melville) said, "We closed the Progressive transaction on January 1" and that "the acquisition adds over $700 million in assets and 9 branches across North Louisiana," with "asset quality of the acquired portfolio" described as "stellar." He also said, "construction on the Meta data center project in Northeast Louisiana has accelerated and been expanded," and "we expect tens of billions of dollars of private investment" in a region where the bank is "well situated to capture the benefits."
  • On recruiting and growth posture in Texas, (CEO Melville) said new Houston market President Jon Heine "has attracted an additional 11 teammates, including 7 production officers" and that the bank added Ben Marmande to lead corporate banking in Texas, stating, "These new partners have already begun building a pipeline of opportunities."
  • On efficiency initiatives, (CEO Melville) said the bank "announced and have begun a partnership with Covecta, a provider of Agentic AI capabilities," starting with "consumer workflows" where the bank "identified over 300 policy rules for potential automation" and expects to expand to "deposits and credit" with "governance, validation and human oversight."
  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Gregory Robertson) reported, "First quarter GAAP net income and EPS available to common shareholders was $22.2 million and $0.68" and "excluding the noncore items, non-GAAP core net income and EPS available to common holders was $24 million and $0.73 per share," adding that results produced "a 1.10% core ROAA and a core efficiency ratio of 62%."

Outlook

  • (CEO Melville) reiterated key targets, saying, "We reiterated full year loan guidance on loan growth" and "we continue to forecast a 1.25% ROAA end-of-year run rate."
  • (CFO Robertson) framed the rate and margin setup, stating, "Our baseline assumption is that we do not receive any further rate cuts in 2026" and "believe we can achieve modest margin improvement in a slightly down or up rate environment."
  • In Q&A, (CFO Robertson) laid out the loan-growth path after a slow start: "we're thinking about high single digits to 10% maybe in the second and third quarter," which he said "would end up offsetting kind of the slow first quarter" toward "5% to 6% range loan growth on an annualized basis."

Financial Results

  • (CFO Robertson) said the Progressive deal "added $774 million in total assets and 9 new locations" and that "total loans held for investment increased $494.8 million or 32% annualized" linked-quarter, while "excluding the acquired Progressive loans, total loans held for investment declined $102.7 million or 6.2% annualized" due to "an overall increase in loan paydowns and payoffs."
  • On balance-sheet funding, (CFO Robertson) reported, "Total deposits increased $766.4 million" and that "excluding acquired Progressive deposits, organic deposit growth was $81.5 million or 4.4% annualized," while the bank "repaid FHLB balances and broker deposits" with "total FHLB borrowings decreased $170.4 million and broker deposits were reduced by $112.5 million."
  • On margin drivers, (CFO Robertson) said "GAAP reported first quarter net interest margin decreased 6 basis points linked quarter to 3.65%" and that "loan discount accretion" was "$1.1 million" due to "lower actual rate marks from the Progressive acquisition," adding, "We would expect quarterly loan discount accretion to be in the low $1 million range for the balance of 2026."
  • On fees and expense discipline, (CEO Melville) highlighted "noninterest income, primarily through the Financial Services group" including "interest rate swaps and SBA loan gains on sale," while (CFO Robertson) said "core noninterest expense for the first quarter was $55.2 million" and that it "did come in lower than we expected" due to timing, and "core noninterest income" was "$13.9 million" driven by "continued strong swap fee revenue and gain on sale from SBA activity."

Q&A

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Asked how much NPAs could come down and what drove the increase; (CFO Robertson) responded, "we think about 30% of the current NPA list will go through to resolution" in Q2, and said the quarterly NPA increase was "about $25 million" and "mostly attributable" to "one client" with "about $16 million of exposure."
  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Asked whether margin should rise; (CFO Robertson) replied, "we think low to mid-single-digit margin expansion as we move forward," noting it is "reliant on moving some of those NPAs back into accruing assets."
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: Asked about loss recognition/allowance and whether charge-offs may rise; (CFO Robertson) said, "we are seeing reserves versus loss recognition going forward to remain pretty consistent with what the Street has forecast for us" and cited comfort from "moving past dues back down below 50 basis points."
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: Asked about paydowns and customer behavior; (CFO Robertson) said "the majority of our paydowns were in the Texas franchise" as some "'22, '23" projects ended and the bank chose "whether it rate or credit to move away from relationships," while (CEO Melville) added, "we've really dramatically downshifted our exposure to construction" and "we certainly are prioritizing the need to get paid for what we do over just loan growth."
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about expense trajectory and Progressive cost saves; (CFO Robertson) said in Q2 "we would expect the mid- to upper 50s" in expenses, while (CEO Melville) said the bank remains "confident" in "achieving most" Progressive savings "in the fourth quarter" and cited "about $11 million" of annualized cost saves expected from a "$21 million Progressive run rate."
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about purchase accounting marks and buybacks; (CFO Robertson) said initial vs. close differences were mainly because "the yield curve was a lot different by the time we closed" and that tangible book impact shifted from an expected "$0.44" dilution to "ex AOCI about $0.04"; (CEO Melville) said buybacks averaged "about 1.19x" tangible book value and that repurchases could take priority, stating, "that will be a higher priority than seeking out M&A opportunities in the near term."
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: Asked about sustaining loan growth despite payoffs and construction appetite; (CFO Robertson) said payoffs should "slightly reduce" as the bank moves away from "'22 and '23 deals," and on construction said, "you can see it bounce in the high single digits to 10% range on a go-forward basis."
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: Asked whether margin had “noise” from interest reversals; (CFO Robertson) answered, "there was about $1.2 million in interest reversal" tied to moving "about $25 million in loans to NPL" and said timing of moving assets back to earning is "a little bit tricky."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on credit and margin durability, including questions that pressed on "how much we could maybe see NPAs come down" (Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group) and whether charge-offs could be "a little bit higher" (Matt Olney, Stephens).
  • Management tone was slightly positive but cautious on credit timing, with (CFO Robertson) caveating resolution forecasting: "the process through moving them to resolution is sometimes precarious and moves at different speeds," while maintaining confidence on margin: "we think low to mid-single-digit margin expansion."
  • Versus the prior quarter, credit remained a focal point, but management’s language shifted from identifying a concentrated driver in Q4 2025 ("largely attributable to the deterioration of a single $25.8 million commercial real estate relationship," said (CFO Robertson) then) to outlining a staged resolution path in Q1 2026 ("we think about 30% of the current NPA list will go through to resolution," said (CFO Robertson) now).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Acquisition narrative moved from anticipation to execution: in Q4 2025, (CEO Melville) said the bank "closed the acquisition of Progressive Bank" "at the turn of the year," while in Q1 2026 he said, "We closed the Progressive transaction on January 1" and emphasized integration progress and footprint expansion.
  • Margin and accretion expectations shifted lower: in Q4 2025, (CFO Robertson) said 2026 accretion was expected to be "approximately $1.8 million" quarterly including Progressive; in Q1 2026 he said accretion was "$1.1 million" and expected to stay "in the low $1 million range" for 2026.
  • Loan-growth messaging stayed mid-single-digit for 2026, but Q1 2026 provided more explicit quarterly cadence: Q4 2025 included, "we still feel very comfortable with the mid-single-digit loan growth" (CFO Robertson), while Q1 2026 added, "high single digits to 10% maybe in the second and third quarter" (CFO Robertson) to offset Q1 paydowns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Credit resolution execution risk remained central: (CFO Robertson) said, "we've kind of been talking about some of these credits for almost a year now" and warned resolution timing can be "precarious."
  • Margin sensitivity to nonaccrual migration was highlighted: (CFO Robertson) tied margin drag to "about $1.2 million in interest reversal" and said reclaiming earning-asset status is "a little bit tricky."
  • Expense trajectory risk was flagged alongside hiring: (CFO Robertson) said expenses could move to "the upper 50s" by year-end, and (CEO Melville) noted new-hire costs would "add incrementally" while expecting production hires to support "operating leverage improvements."

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a strong quarter supported by the Progressive acquisition close, tighter cost execution than expected, and meaningful noninterest income from swaps and SBA gains. They reiterated a full-year stance built around sustained loan-growth expectations and a "1.25% ROAA end-of-year run rate," while acknowledging that elevated paydowns and credit migration—along with interest reversals tied to nonaccruals—will shape near-term margin and earnings variability until resolutions progress.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. Army to proceed with delayed General Dynamics ammunition plant - Bloomberg
data.description:
The U.S. Army will move ahead with a $591M General Dynamics (GD) contract to start production at an artillery and ammunition factory in Texas after severe delays prompted a review of whether the project should be canceled, Bloomberg reported Monday.

A work stoppage order on the factory was lifted after eight months—it is not clear why the pause went into effect or why it was lifted—and the Army said it is now working on "the way forward" with General Dynamics (GD).

The plant, which opened in May 2024 and was meant to have three lines making 30K 155 mm artillery shells per month, has not yet started production, after an Army letter to General Dynamics (GD) in June 2025 said the equipment at the site failed to meet the "technical requirements of the contract," according to reports at the time.

Asked why work was ordered halted at the plant, the Army cited "performance concerns, risk allocation, and the need for contract modifications" in a statement to Bloomberg.

General Dynamics (GD) reports Q1 earnings this week and may offer more detail on the future of the plant and capital spending it plans to make to improve the production process, Bloomberg reported.

data.title: Banco Latinoamericano GAAP EPS of $1.31, revenue of $83.1M
data.description:
• Banco Latinoamericano press release (BLX): Q1 GAAP EPS of $1.31.
  • Revenue of $83.1M (+6.7% Y/Y).

data.title: Veradermics announces launch of public offering
data.description:
• Veradermics (MANE) on Monday announced that it has launched a proposed public offering of 3.35M shares of common stock.
  • All of the shares and pre-funded warrants are being offered by Veradermics.
  • In addition, the underwriters are expected to have a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 502,500 shares of common stock at the public offering price, less underwriting discounts and commissions, solely with respect to shares of common stock and pre-funded warrants sold in the public offering.
  • MANE -4.75% after hours to $95.34.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Irenic Acquisition prices $220M IPO, Nasdaq trading begins
data.description:
• Irenic Acquisition (IACQU) priced its IPO of 22M units at $10 per unit, raising $220M.
  • Each unit includes one Class A share and one-third of a warrant exercisable at $11.50.
  • Units begin trading on Nasdaq under “IACQU” on April 28, 2026.
  • Shares and warrants will trade separately as “IACQ” and “IACQW” after separation.
  • The IPO is expected to close on April 29, 2026.
  • The SPAC will target deals in aerospace, defense, and industrial sectors.

data.title: Novo Nordisk gains Fast Track status for coramitug to treat ATTR amyloidosis
data.description:
• The US FDA has granted Fast Track designation to coramitug, under development by Novo Nordisk (NVO) for ATTR amyloidosis with cardiomyopathy (ATTR-CM).
  • The amyloid depleter antibody is currently in the phase 3 CLEOPATTRA trial, which is expected to achieve primary completion in 2029.
  • Novo Nordisk acquired global rights to coramitug (PRX004) in a July 2021 deal with Prothena Corp. (PRTA), which received $100M upfront, with the transaction value worth up to $1.2B with milestone payments.

data.title: Dollar dips ahead of key central bank decisions, Iran tensions linger: Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. dollar edged lower on Monday, with investors cautious amid uncertainty over U.S.-Iran developments and ahead of key central bank meetings this week.

The dollar index (DXY), which measures the greenback against a basket of major currencies, was last down 0.06% to $98.47 but remains up about 0.25% for the year to date.

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers:

Over the past week, the index advanced 0.29%, with the greenback’s trajectory shaped by shifting expectations around the U.S.-Iran conflict and evolving central bank outlooks.

Early in the period, the greenback found support as tensions in the Middle East escalated, with Iran tightening control over the Strait of Hormuz and seizing vessels, raising concerns over prolonged disruptions to global energy supplies and prompting safe-haven demand.

This strength was reinforced by a growing consensus that major central banks would adopt a wait-and-see approach, as policymakers assessed the inflationary implications of higher oil prices. Expectations that the Federal Reserve and its global peers would hold rates steady, alongside a resilient U.S. economic backdrop, further underpinned the dollar.

However, sentiment remained fluid, and the dollar came under pressure at times as diplomatic efforts to revive talks resurfaced, with reports of a new Iranian proposal and continued mediation keeping markets cautious.

Later in the week, the dollar’s gains moderated as the market grappled with competing forces, persistent geopolitical risks supporting safe-haven flows, while shifting expectations around U.S. monetary policy, including the potential transition in Federal Reserve leadership, introduced a more dovish undertone.

By the end of the period, the dollar edged lower as traders positioned cautiously ahead of a heavy slate of central bank meetings, including what is expected to be Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s final policy decision, with markets bracing for guidance on the economic fallout from the ongoing conflict.

During the period, global markets were influenced by developments in the U.S.-Iran conflict, fluctuations in oil prices, evolving inflation expectations, and anticipation of key central bank policy signals.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rising nine basis points to 4.35%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose seven basis points to 3.81%.

Currency Highlights:

Major currency movements (April 20 to April 26)

Euro (EUR:USD)

-0.39%

Pound Sterling (GBP:USD)

+0.20%

Japanese Yen (JPY:USD)

-0.16%

Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD)

-0.20%

Swiss Franc (CHF:USD)

-0.73%

Australian Dollar (AUD:USD)

+0.10%

Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD)

-0.13%

The euro (EUR:USD) was last down 0.01%, with the currency trading in a narrow range as European markets remained mixed amid persistent geopolitical tensions and uncertainty around U.S.-Iran developments.

Sentiment was further weighed by weak economic data, with Euro Area composite PMI slipping into contraction territory, while investors stayed cautious ahead of key central bank decisions.

The pound sterling (GBP:USD) was last up 0.01%, holding steady as investors weighed mixed domestic signals alongside broader geopolitical uncertainty.

U.K. economic data showed inflation rising and unemployment easing, while sentiment remained cautious amid global developments and ahead of policy direction.

The Swiss franc (CHF:USD) was last down 0.08%, extending its weekly decline as the currency remained under pressure amid broader market uncertainty.
